title: The Primary and Secondary Corporate Credit Facilities

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This research paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York documents the design, operations, and impact of the Primary Market Corporate Credit Facility (PMCCF) and Secondary Market Corporate Credit Facility (SMCCF)—the Fed's first-ever intervention in corporate bond markets. The facilities were launched in March 2020 to address severe market dislocations during the COVID-19 pandemic, including yield spread widening of approximately 250 bps for investment-grade bonds and 550 bps for high-yield bonds, and nearly $200 billion in outflows from corporate bond funds. The SMCCF made total purchases of $14 billion before ceasing operations on December 31, 2020.
key_points: The Federal Reserve announced the PMCCF and SMCCF on March 23, 2020—the Fed's first-ever intervention in corporate bond markets under Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act
SMCCF commenced ETF purchases on May 12, 2020, and bond purchases on June 16, 2020; the PMCCF became operational on June 29, 2020 but was never used by any borrower
The SMCCF ceased purchases on December 31, 2020, after making total purchases of $14 billion; portfolio wind-down was completed by August 31, 2021

In the last two weeks of March 2020, corporate bond mutual funds and ETFs experienced almost $200 billion in outflows, with bid-ask spreads deteriorating to levels not seen since the global financial crisis

High-yield corporate bond issuance halted almost entirely between March 4 and April 16, 2020; new issue concessions reached approximately 50+ bps above secondary market yields (roughly double the usual level)
The facilities' goal was to support market liquidity and credit availability for large employers by providing a backstop to investment-grade corporate debt issuers
why_it_matters: This paper is a primary source documenting how the Federal Reserve deployed emergency credit facilities to address corporate bond market dysfunction during a systemic crisis. Understanding the design, operational timeline, and measured impact of these facilities provides critical institutional knowledge for evaluating future market stress responses and the Fed's evolving role as a backstop to credit markets.
macro_context: The corporate bond market dislocations in March 2020 threatened to cut off access to credit for U.S. employers dependent on debt capital markets. The Fed's intervention helped restore market functioning, enabling companies to continue employing workers and funding pandemic-related working capital and investment needs.
market_structure_context: The paper details the OTC (over-the-counter) structure of the secondary corporate bond market where investors interact with dealers, and documents how the SMCCF operated within this structure by purchasing both ETFs and individual bonds. The authors analyze how the facilities addressed bid-ask spread deterioration and liquidity fragmentation that emerged during the March 2020 crisis.
important_numbers: $9.6 trillion in corporate debt securities outstanding as of end of 2019
~250 bps yield spread increase for investment-grade bonds in March 2020
~550 bps yield spread increase for high-yield bonds in March 2020
~$200 billion in outflows from corporate bond funds in last two weeks of March 2020
~$50+ bps new issue concession (approximately double the usual level)
notes: Historical research paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York documenting the Fed's first intervention in corporate bond markets. Published June 2022 (46 months ago), making it a historical record. Despite the age, this is a high-quality primary source from a trusted institutional domain (ny.fed.org). The paper provides comprehensive documentation of market structure dynamics, including OTC trading, dealer intermediation, and liquidity conditions. The SMCCF was a significant precedent for Fed involvement in credit markets, with direct implications for understanding future emergency facilities. The PMCCF was never used, which is itself a notable finding about the signaling effect of Fed backstop facilities. Source PDF was truncated during extraction; some detailed analysis sections may be incomplete.
